== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These burial mounds date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 burial mounds, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These burial mounds are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

revision.text:
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

revision.text:
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}
<!--Sugitani Ichiban-Tsuka Kofun--> <!--Sugitani Ichiban-Tsuka Kofun-->

This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=draft}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=draft}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=draft}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

revision.comment: 外部リンクの修正 http:// -> https:// (www.city.toyama.toyama.jp) ([[Wikipedia:Bot|Bot]]による編集)
revision.text:
{{Location map|Japan Toyama|lat_deg=36|lat_min=40|lat_sec=44.75|lon_deg=137|lon_min=8|lon_sec=40.28|label=杉谷古墳群|position=top|width=220|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|杉谷古墳群の位置}}}} {{Maplink2|zoom=12|frame=yes|frame-align=right|frame-width=260|frame-height=200|frame-latitude=36.691723|frame-longitude=137.174192 |type=point|coord={{Coord|36|40|44.75|N|137|8|40.28|E}}|marker=1 |type2=point|coord2={{Coord|36|40|41.64|N|137|10|8.23|E}}|marker2=2 |type3=point|coord3={{Coord|36|42|4.4|N|137|12|47.4|E}}|marker3=3 |text='''1.'''杉谷古墳群、'''2.'''[[婦中鵜坂駅]]、'''3.'''[[富山駅]]}} {{座標一覧}} '''杉谷古墳群'''（すぎたにこふんぐん）は、[[富山県]][[富山市]]杉谷にある[[古墳群]]。史跡指定はされていない。 == 概要 == 富山県中央部、[[富山平野]]を東西に二分する[[呉羽丘陵]]から発達する、杉谷丘陵の縁辺部に営造された[[古墳時代]]初頭頃の古墳群である{{Sfn|杉谷古墳群（古墳）|1989}}{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。[[前方後方墳]]1基（一番塚）・[[四隅突出型墳丘墓|四隅突出墳]]1基（4号墳）・[[円墳]]1基（三番塚）・[[方墳]]または長方墳7基・不明1基の計11基から構成される{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。現在は[[富山大学]]杉谷キャンパス（旧[[富山医科薬科大学]]）を取り巻く位置に分布し、これまでに測量・発掘調査が実施されている。 古墳群のうち特に4号墳は、方墳の四隅が舌状に突出する四隅突出墳（四隅突出型墳丘墓/四隅突出型方墳）という特異な形状として注目され、四隅突出墳としては最大級の規模になる。四隅突出墳は[[山陰地方]]の[[島根県]]を中心に分布する[[弥生時代]]-古墳時代初頭の墓制であるが、[[北陸地方]]でも18基（[[福井県]]6基、[[石川県]]2基、富山県10基）が確認され、いずれも外表施設に貼石を持たない点で山陰地方とは異なる特徴を示しており、日本海文化圏の実態を考察するうえで重要な資料になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}[https://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm 杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡]（富山市ホームページ）。。関連して5号墳と4号墳の間（大学への進入路）の杉谷A遺跡では、古墳時代初頭の方形周溝墓17基・円形周溝墓1基・土壙2基などの墳墓群が確認されているが、杉谷古墳群はその上位の首長墓群と想定され、当時の首長系譜を考察するうえで重要視される古墳群になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}} == 遺跡歴 == * 『越中志徴』に「糠塚」の記述{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）、「富山県石器時代遺跡地名表」に縄文遺跡として登録{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1973年]]度（昭和48年度）、一-三番塚・4-6号墳の発掘調査、7号墳の発見・発掘調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1974年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1974年度（昭和49年度）、[[富山医科薬科大学]]建設に伴う杉谷A遺跡の発掘調査。周溝墓群の発見（富山市教育委員会、[[1975年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1975年（昭和50年）、富山医科薬科大学設置（[[2005年]]に[[富山大学]]に統合）。 * [[1983年]]度（昭和58年度）、一・二番塚・8-10号墳の測量・発掘調査、4号墳の測量調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1984年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[2010年|2010]]-[[2011年]]度（[[平成]]22-23年度）、6号墳の発掘調査（計2次、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2012]]・[[2013年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2012年|2012]]-[[2017年]]度（平成24-29年度）、4・7号墳の発掘調査（計6次：7号墳は2017年度のみ、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2014]]-[[2019年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2018年|2018]]-[[2019年]]度（平成30-[[令和]]元年度）、一番塚の測量・発掘調査（富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2020年|2020]]・[[2021年]]に報告書刊行）。 == 一覧 == [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-1, funkyu.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|一番塚古墳}}]] == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|12=draft}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama City|ja|富山市|12=draft}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=draft}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=draft}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=draft}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|ja|富山大学|12=draft}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|ja|島根県|12=draft}}, where they were in use from the [[Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=draft}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=draft}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=draft}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

'''杉谷古墳群'''（すぎたにこふんぐん）は、[[富山県]][[富山市]]杉谷にある[[古墳群]]。史跡指定はされていない。 == 概要 == 富山県中央部、[[富山平野]]を東西に二分する[[呉羽丘陵]]から発達する、杉谷丘陵の縁辺部に営造された[[古墳時代]]初頭頃の古墳群である{{Sfn|杉谷古墳群（古墳）|1989}}{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。[[前方後方墳]]1基（一番塚）・[[四隅突出型墳丘墓|四隅突出墳]]1基（4号墳）・[[円墳]]1基（三番塚）・[[方墳]]または長方墳7基・不明1基の計11基から構成される{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。現在は[[富山大学]]杉谷キャンパス（旧[[富山医科薬科大学]]）を取り巻く位置に分布し、これまでに測量・発掘調査が実施されている。 古墳群のうち特に4号墳は、方墳の四隅が舌状に突出する四隅突出墳（四隅突出型墳丘墓/四隅突出型方墳）という特異な形状として注目され、四隅突出墳としては最大級の規模になる。四隅突出墳は[[山陰地方]]の[[島根県]]を中心に分布する[[弥生時代]]-古墳時代初頭の墓制であるが、[[北陸地方]]でも18基（[[福井県]]6基、[[石川県]]2基、富山県10基）が確認され、いずれも外表施設に貼石を持たない点で山陰地方とは異なる特徴を示しており、日本海文化圏の実態を考察するうえで重要な資料になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}[https://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm 杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡]（富山市ホームページ）。。関連して5号墳と4号墳の間（大学への進入路）の杉谷A遺跡では、古墳時代初頭の方形周溝墓17基・円形周溝墓1基・土壙2基などの墳墓群が確認されているが、杉谷古墳群はその上位の首長墓群と想定され、当時の首長系譜を考察するうえで重要視される古墳群になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}} == 遺跡歴 == * 『越中志徴』に「糠塚」の記述{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）、「富山県石器時代遺跡地名表」に縄文遺跡として登録{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1973年]]度（昭和48年度）、一-三番塚・4-6号墳の発掘調査、7号墳の発見・発掘調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1974年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1974年度（昭和49年度）、[[富山医科薬科大学]]建設に伴う杉谷A遺跡の発掘調査。周溝墓群の発見（富山市教育委員会、[[1975年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1975年（昭和50年）、富山医科薬科大学設置（[[2005年]]に[[富山大学]]に統合）。 * [[1983年]]度（昭和58年度）、一・二番塚・8-10号墳の測量・発掘調査、4号墳の測量調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1984年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[2010年|2010]]-[[2011年]]度（[[平成]]22-23年度）、6号墳の発掘調査（計2次、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2012]]・[[2013年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2012年|2012]]-[[2017年]]度（平成24-29年度）、4・7号墳の発掘調査（計6次：7号墳は2017年度のみ、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2014]]-[[2019年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2018年|2018]]-[[2019年]]度（平成30-[[令和]]元年度）、一番塚の測量・発掘調査（富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2020年|2020]]・[[2021年]]に報告書刊行）。 == 一覧 == [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-1, funkyu.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|一番塚古墳}}]] {{Location map|Japan Toyama|lat_deg=36|lat_min=40|lat_sec=44.75|lon_deg=137|lon_min=8|lon_sec=40.28|label=杉谷古墳群|position=top|width=220|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|caption={{Center|杉谷古墳群の位置}}}} {{Maplink2|zoom=12|frame=yes|frame-align=right|frame-width=260|frame-height=200|frame-latitude=36.691723|frame-longitude=137.174192 |type=point|coord={{Coord|36|40|44.75|N|137|8|40.28|E}}|marker=1 |type2=point|coord2={{Coord|36|40|41.64|N|137|10|8.23|E}}|marker2=2 |type3=point|coord3={{Coord|36|42|4.4|N|137|12|47.4|E}}|marker3=3 |text='''1.'''杉谷古墳群、'''2.'''[[婦中鵜坂駅]]、'''3.'''[[富山駅]]}} {{座標一覧}} '''杉谷古墳群'''（すぎたにこふんぐん）は、[[富山県]][[富山市]]杉谷にある[[古墳群]]。史跡指定はされていない。 == 概要 == 富山県中央部、[[富山平野]]を東西に二分する[[呉羽丘陵]]から発達する、杉谷丘陵の縁辺部に営造された[[古墳時代]]初頭頃の古墳群である{{Sfn|杉谷古墳群（古墳）|1989}}{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。[[前方後方墳]]1基（一番塚）・[[四隅突出型墳丘墓|四隅突出墳]]1基（4号墳）・[[円墳]]1基（三番塚）・[[方墳]]または長方墳7基・不明1基の計11基から構成される{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。現在は[[富山大学]]杉谷キャンパス（旧[[富山医科薬科大学]]）を取り巻く位置に分布し、これまでに測量・発掘調査が実施されている。 古墳群のうち特に4号墳は、方墳の四隅が舌状に突出する四隅突出墳（四隅突出型墳丘墓/四隅突出型方墳）という特異な形状として注目され、四隅突出墳としては最大級の規模になる。四隅突出墳は[[山陰地方]]の[[島根県]]を中心に分布する[[弥生時代]]-古墳時代初頭の墓制であるが、[[北陸地方]]でも18基（[[福井県]]6基、[[石川県]]2基、富山県10基）が確認され、いずれも外表施設に貼石を持たない点で山陰地方とは異なる特徴を示しており、日本海文化圏の実態を考察するうえで重要な資料になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}[https://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm 杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡]（富山市ホームページ）。。関連して5号墳と4号墳の間（大学への進入路）の杉谷A遺跡では、古墳時代初頭の方形周溝墓17基・円形周溝墓1基・土壙2基などの墳墓群が確認されているが、杉谷古墳群はその上位の首長墓群と想定され、当時の首長系譜を考察するうえで重要視される古墳群になる{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}} == 遺跡歴 == * 『越中志徴』に「糠塚」の記述{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1950年]]（[[昭和]]25年）、「富山県石器時代遺跡地名表」に縄文遺跡として登録{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[1973年]]度（昭和48年度）、一-三番塚・4-6号墳の発掘調査、7号墳の発見・発掘調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1974年]]に概報刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1974年度（昭和49年度）、[[富山医科薬科大学]]建設に伴う杉谷A遺跡の発掘調査。周溝墓群の発見（富山市教育委員会、[[1975年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * 1975年（昭和50年）、富山医科薬科大学設置（[[2005年]]に[[富山大学]]に統合）。 * [[1983年]]度（昭和58年度）、一・二番塚・8-10号墳の測量・発掘調査、4号墳の測量調査（富山市教育委員会、[[1984年]]に報告書刊行）{{Sfn|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|2010}}。 * [[2010年|2010]]-[[2011年]]度（[[平成]]22-23年度）、6号墳の発掘調査（計2次、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2012]]・[[2013年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2012年|2012]]-[[2017年]]度（平成24-29年度）、4・7号墳の発掘調査（計6次：7号墳は2017年度のみ、富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2012年|2014]]-[[2019年]]に報告書刊行）。 * [[2018年|2018]]-[[2019年]]度（平成30-[[令和]]元年度）、一番塚の測量・発掘調査（富山大学人文学部考古学研究室、[[2020年|2020]]・[[2021年]]に報告書刊行）。 == 一覧 == [[File:Sugitani Kofun Group-1, funkyu.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|一番塚古墳}}]] （56m）||style="text-align:left"|1973年度：発掘

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Toyama|qid=Q204266}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.67909722|N|137.14452222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q60784932]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}
{{Kofun navbar}}
{{translated page|ja|杉谷古墳群|version=104433487|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Toyama|WD=Q204266}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.67909722|N|137.14452222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q60784932]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}
