<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Toyama|WD=Q204266}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.67909722|N|137.14452222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q60784932]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Toyama|WD=Q204266}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.67909722|N|137.14452222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q60784932]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in [[:en:Toyama (city)|Toyama City]], Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of [[:en:University of Toyama|University of Toyama]] (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], where they were in use from the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]] to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Sugitani Kofun Group]][[ja:杉谷古墳群]] {{Infobox ancient site|location={{ill|Toyama|WD=Q204266}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.67909722|N|137.14452222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Sugitani Kofun Group}} * [http://www.city.toyama.toyama.jp/etc/maibun/center/topics/stkf/stkf.htm {{ill|Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja|杉谷古墳群・杉谷A遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Kofun Group and Sugitani A Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] - Toyama City Homepage
== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama (city)|en|Toyama (city)|WD=Q204266|lt=Toyama City}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|en|University of Toyama|WD=Q1147924|lt=University of Toyama}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture}}, where they were in use from the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period}} to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
== References == * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷6号墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani 6-gōfun First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=2020|chapter=|title={{ill|Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja|杉谷1番塚古墳 第1次発掘調査報告書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugitani Ichiban-tsuka First Excavation Report|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|publisher=University of Toyama Archaeology Department}}

== Sugitani Kofun Group == The '''Sugitani Kofun Group''' (杉谷古墳群) is located in {{ill|Toyama (city)|en|Toyama (city)|WD=Q204266|lt=Toyama City|1=Toyama (city)}}, Toyama Prefecture, Japan. These Kofuns date back to the early [[Kofun period]] and were constructed along the Sugitani Hills, which extend from the Kureha Hills that divide the [[Toyama Plain]]. They are currently not designated as a historic site.
The kofun group consists of 11 Kofuns, including one {{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Ichiban-tsuka), one {{ill|square tomb with protruding corners|ja|四隅突出墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square tomb with protruding corners|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (4-gōfun), one {{ill|circular mound|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular mound|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Sanban-tsuka), seven square or rectangular mounds, and one mound of unknown type. These Kofuns are situated around the Sugitani Campus of {{ill|University of Toyama|en|University of Toyama|WD=Q1147924|lt=University of Toyama|1=University of Toyama}} (formerly Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University). Surveys and excavations have been conducted on the site.
Particularly notable is the 4-gōfun, a square tomb with protruding corners. This unique tomb style is more commonly associated with the [[San’in Region]], especially {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|WD=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}, where they were in use from the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|WD=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} to the early Kofun period. However, the North Japan Sea region, including Toyama Prefecture, is home to 18 similar tombs, with Toyama accounting for 10. Unlike their counterparts in San’in, these tombs lack stone coverings on their exteriors, reflecting regional characteristics of the Japan Sea cultural sphere.
== History == * Listed in the Edo-period text {{ill|Echū Shichō|ja|越中志徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echū Shichō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} under the name "Nukazuka." * 1950: Registered as a Jōmon archaeological site in the Toyama Prefecture List of Stone Age Sites. * 1973: Excavations conducted on Ichiban-tsuka, Sanban-tsuka, 4-gōfun, 6-gōfun, and the discovery of 7-gōfun. * 1975: Construction of Toyama Medical and Pharmaceutical University (now part of the University of Toyama). * 1983: Survey and excavation of Ichiban-tsuka and 8–10-gōfun. * 2010–2011: Excavation of 6-gōfun conducted by the University of Toyama’s Archaeology Department. * 2012–2017: Excavation of 4-gōfun and 7-gōfun.
